annotations.label: Randomness (common usage)

anchor.snippet: randomness is the apparent or actual lack of definite patterns or predictability in information

annotations.note: This is an informal/philosophical characterization of randomness with no corresponding Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Throwing two dice

anchor.snippet: the outcome of any particular roll is unpredictable, but a sum of 7 will tend to occur twice as often as 4

annotations.note: The specific dice-sum example is not a Mathlib declaration, though uniform PMFs could model individual dice.

annotations.label: Random variable

anchor.snippet: a random variable is an assignment of a numerical value to each possible outcome of an event space

annotations.note: Mathlib encodes random variables as measurable functions on a probability space rather than via a dedicated type, with predicates like HasLaw registering their law.

annotations.label: Random process

anchor.snippet: A random process is a sequence of random variables whose outcomes do not follow a deterministic pattern

annotations.note: Stochastic processes are represented as families `ι → Ω → E` with supporting infrastructure (filtrations, adaptedness) rather than a single named definition.

annotations.label: Random selection / simple random sample

anchor.snippet: Random selection, when narrowly associated with a simple random sample

annotations.note: The uniform distribution over a set (IsUniform / PMF.uniformOfFinset) captures equal-probability random selection but not the survey-sampling notion of a simple random sample.

annotations.label: Bowl of marbles

anchor.snippet: with a bowl containing just 10 red marbles and 90 blue marbles

annotations.note: This is a concrete illustrative example with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Ramsey theory: pure randomness impossible

anchor.snippet: pure randomness (in the sense of there being no discernible pattern) is impossible

annotations.note: The Ramsey-theoretic results underlying this claim (Hales–Jewett) are formalized, but the informal statement that pure randomness is impossible is not itself a theorem.

annotations.label: Infinite hierarchy of randomness

anchor.snippet: there is infinite hierarchy (in terms of quality or strength) of forms of randomness

annotations.note: The hierarchy of algorithmic randomness notions is not developed in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kolmogorov randomness

anchor.snippet: a string of bits is random if and only if it is shorter than any computer program that can produce that string

annotations.note: Kolmogorov complexity and Kolmogorov randomness are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Martin-Löf randomness

anchor.snippet: An infinite sequence is random if and only if it withstands all recursively enumerable null sets

annotations.note: Martin-Löf randomness and recursively enumerable null sets are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Recursive and Schnorr randomness

anchor.snippet: recursive randomness and Schnorr randomness, which are based on recursively computable martingales

annotations.note: Measure-theoretic martingales are formalized, but the computable-martingale-based notions of recursive and Schnorr randomness are not.

annotations.label: Wang: randomness notions differ

anchor.snippet: It was shown by Yongge Wang that these randomness notions are generally different

annotations.note: This separation result in algorithmic randomness theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Digit frequencies of pi

anchor.snippet: In the first six billion decimal places of pi, each of the digits from 0 through 9 shows up about six hundred million times

annotations.note: Empirical digit-frequency statistics and the normality of pi are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Random walk hypothesis

anchor.snippet: asset prices in an organized market evolve at random, in the sense that the expected value of their change is zero

annotations.note: The martingale property (zero expected increment given the past) is formalized as MeasureTheory.Martingale, which generalizes the financial random-walk hypothesis.

annotations.label: Three mechanisms of random behavior

anchor.snippet: there exist three mechanisms responsible for (apparently) random behavior in systems

annotations.note: This is an informal taxonomy of sources of randomness with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Pseudorandomness

anchor.snippet: This is also called pseudorandomness

annotations.note: Pseudorandomness and pseudorandom generators are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Random processes have no memory

anchor.snippet: Truly random processes such as these do not have memory, which makes it impossible for past outcomes to affect future outcomes

annotations.note: The 'no memory' property for independent trials is captured by ProbabilityTheory.IndepFun, but memorylessness is not stated as a standalone theorem.

annotations.label: Drawing a jack without replacement

anchor.snippet: once a jack is removed from the deck, the next draw is less likely to be a jack and more likely to be some other card

annotations.note: This sampling-without-replacement example is not formalized, though conditional probability (ProbabilityTheory.cond) exists as background machinery.

annotations.label: Fair die: past rolls uninformative

anchor.snippet: If the die is known to be fair, then previous rolls can give no indication of future events

annotations.note: The claim is exactly statistical independence of trials, formalized as ProbabilityTheory.IndepFun, though not stated as this specific fair-die proposition.

annotations.label: Two-children problem

anchor.snippet: one would notice that the probability is actually only

annotations.note: The two-children probability puzzle is not formalized, though the conditional-probability tools it uses (ProbabilityTheory.cond) exist.

annotations.label: Monty Hall problem

anchor.snippet: a game show scenario in which a car is hidden behind one of three doors

annotations.note: A grep for Monty Hall found nothing; the puzzle is not formalized, though Bayes' theorem (cond_eq_inv_mul_cond_mul) is available.
